label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, this is a philosophical question about causation and intention. Let's break it down. Brown wants to kill Smith. Smith is in a different building. There's a bomb in that building. This bomb can only be detonated by producing a six-dotted image on the lens of a camera focused on the top of a table in Brown's room. So, Brown takes a normal, fair six-sided die and throws it onto the table, hoping it will land with six facing up. He throws a six and the bomb detonates, killing Smith. The question is: Did Brown intentionally roll a six? This seems like it's testing the difference between intention and desire. Brown's ultimate desire is to kill Smith. He knows that to kill Smith, he needs to detonate the bomb. He knows that to detonate the bomb, he needs to produce a six-dotted image. So he throws a die onto the table, hoping it will land six-up. Now, did he intentionally roll a six? If intention refers to the purpose behind the action, then Brown's ultimate purpose is to kill Smith.
